name: Ontario Science Centre, Toronto
concept&appearance: Designed by architect Raymond Moriyama in a Brutalist style, the centre consists of three main buildings (Entrance Building, Core Building, and Valley Building) connected by a series of bridges and escalators that descend into the Don River ravine. The design philosophy emphasized a 'sequence of experiences' and a strong connection with the natural environment, using the rugged ravine landscape as an integral part of the visitor journey. The structure features rough-textured concrete (board-marked), limestone, and large glass expanses to blend with the surrounding forest.
permanent_exhibitions: The museum featured several core permanent galleries: 1. The Living Earth (Level 6): A simulated rainforest environment and exhibits on biodiversity and coral reefs. 2. Space Hall (Level 4): Featured Toronto's only public planetarium, a Mars meteorite, and a Moon rock. 3. The AstraZeneca Human Edge (Level 6): Explored the limits of the human body and biology. 4. KidSpark (Level 4): A popular learn-through-play space for children under eight. 5. The Science Arcade (Level 6): One of the oldest exhibits, featuring classic hands-on physics and electricity demonstrations. 6. The Weston Family Innovation Centre (Level 6): Focused on experimentation and problem-solving. 7. A Question of Truth (Level 5): Examined bias in science and the scientific method. 8. Cohon Family Nature Escape: An outdoor exhibit in the ravine.
central_hall: The Great Hall was a vast, triangular event space located in the Core Building. It featured high ceilings, natural light, and 'Cloud', a massive computer-controlled kinetic sculpture by David Rokeby consisting of an array of suspended blue and transparent squares that rotated to simulate the states of matter.
special_effects_theaters: OMNIMAX Theatre: Ontario's only IMAX Dome theatre, opened in 1996, featuring a dome screen that wrapped around the audience, 320 seats, and 13,000 watts of sound, projecting 70mm film (15/70 format).
science_popularization_activities: The centre operated the Ontario Science Centre Science School (offering Grade 12 science credits), hosted 'Sleepovers' for youth groups and families, ran summer day camps, and provided daily science demonstrations and planetarium shows. It also hosted a demonstrative Amateur Radio Station.
